text: Zeno of Citium, founding the Stoa around 300 BCE, declared that the highest human good was living 'in agreement with nature' — homologoumenos te phusei zen. But Zeno did not mean nature in a pastoral sense, retreating to forests or rivers. He meant the rational nature specific to human beings: logos, the capacity to reason coherently and govern impulse. An animal lives by instinct; a plant by growth. The human being alone possesses a governing faculty — the hegemonikon — capable of standing apart from raw desire and choosing deliberately. To live unnaturally, for Zeno, was not to eat poorly but to reason poorly.
question: According to the passage, what did Zeno mean by 'living according to nature'?

options.text: Exercising the rational faculty specific to human beings
Spending time in natural environments away from cities
Following instinctual drives as animals do
Avoiding unnatural foods and artificial substances
